title: Attempting to stop a Quadlet-generated container with no init process using systemctl hangs
body:
### Issue Description

I've installed Podman 4.4.2 on a Ubuntu 22.04 server to run rootless containers, with systemd user service files generated by Quadlet. I took the trivial `mysleep` example from https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/quadlet-podman to test this. (Only modification: adding `Network=pasta`, as I haven't installed `slirp4netns` on this box at all and intend to use `pasta` going forward.)

The container is generated correctly and runs correctly, and comes up on reboots as it should. However, `systemctl --user stop mysleep` hangs, "never" stopping the container (read: not for a very long time). Obviously, `systemctl --user restart mysleep` doesn't work either. After a while, I'll hit Ctrl-C to get back to the shell.

The output of `podman ps` shows the container still running after an attempted stop/restart using `systemctl`. If I then use `podman stop` to stop it, this happens:

```
$ podman stop 3c90228290e7
WARN[0010] StopSignal SIGTERM failed to stop container systemd-mysleep in 10 seconds, resorting to SIGKILL 
3c90228290e7
```

The problem appears to be that the `sleep` binary doesn't respond to SIGTERM, and the Quadlet-generated service file does not resort to SIGKILL after a timeout (or the timeout is so long that few people have the patience to wait for it). If I add `RunInit=true` to the .container file, `systemctl --user stop mysleep` works correctly as `/usr/libexec/podman/catatonit` handles SIGTERM properly.

Honestly, I'm not sure if this should be a feature request or a bug, but in practical terms as an end user, this sure feels like a bug (or an omission in the Quadlet-generated service file). Should the `ExecStopPost` `podman rm` command in the generated service perhaps use the `--time, -t=seconds` parameter and expose it as a configurable option in the `.container` file? It already uses the prerequisite `--force, -f` parameter, after all.

Ultimately it's the user's job to ensure that PID 1 in the container can handle SIGTERM, but this is sure to trip up users with less Linux experience, and part of my reasoning to create the issue is to help people searching for information on this in the future in case this is closed as a "wontfix". This might be a good addition to the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Set up and run the mysleep container as described in the description
2. Run `systemctl --user stop mysleep` or `systemctl --user restart mysleep`


### Describe the results you received

The `stop` command hangs, the container/service is not stopped in a timely fashion.

### Describe the results you expected

After a set timeout (e.g. the 10 seconds used by `podman stop`), the service should just be forcibly SIGKILLed.

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body: this is a known issue with the sleep program not handling SIGTERM.  The container engine or systemd cannot know in advance if the container process can handle that, so the current behavior of timing out after 10 seconds and using SIGKILL is correct.  `sleep` as PID 1 is not a good idea as it is also not able to reap zombies.
